chapters.title: “The economy must be economical“
chapters.text:
On February 20, 1970, the Soviet authorities decided to create a nuclear power plant. Crews of builders began construction near Prypiat in the Kyiv region. The management named the atomic power plant in honor of the area's regional center, Chornobyl (The Ukrainian version of the name is preferred). No one could even imagine that this name would soon become known to the whole world in connection with the worst disaster in the history of nuclear energy.

It is impossible to tell the story of the Chornobyl accident outside the context of domestic and foreign Soviet policy. Therefore, in the titles of the key points you will find famous slogans of the USSR. Here they are used ironically.

In the 1970s, nuclear energy was the USSR's way to rejig the stationary economy and catch up with the West.

The post of station director went to chief engineer Viktor Bryukhanov. He was to lead the construction of the first nuclear power plant in Ukraine from the stage of its blueprints. Bryukhanov calculated all the expenses for the materials necessary for the construction and made estimates. He handed the data over to the State Bank in Kyiv: 400 million rubles.

When the construction began, most engineers moved their wives and children to Prypiat, which was slowly but surely becoming a full-fledged city. Unfortunately, Bryukhanov could not keep up with the plans the authorities entrusted him with. All the expected deadlines for completing the work were unrealistic in practice. Senseless bureaucracy, corruption, and embezzlement reigned around the construction site. In the following years, he had to overcome many difficulties and obstacles.

But in the spring of 1986, the Chornobyl nuclear power plant was one of the best in the Soviet Union, and it was to receive the highest award on May 1, the Order of Lenin. The authorities also planned to award Bryukhanov the Hero of Socialist Labor title.

Alas, May 1986 never arrived in Prypiat. For the next two decades, life there froze in the state in which it was found on the morning of April 26.

chapters.title: “Catch up and overtake“
chapters.text:
On April 25, Prypiat already smelled of summer. The working week was ending, and all the locals were preparing for a long May Day holiday weekend.

Station workers and security teams were finishing their work shifts while their families were stocking up on food for the holiday table and planning activities for Saturday. That evening, everyone was relaxed: the planned shutdown of the fourth power unit was only for the technical inspection, and it was already coming to an end. The engineers carried out such scheduled inspections regularly. As a rule, they stopped power units for testing this or that equipment. This time, the goal of shutting down the power unit was to experiment with the operation of the tube generator as an additional emergency power supply system. It was the fourth such experiment in the previous four years. Although all prior attempts were unsuccessful, the researchers expected a different result this time. But not the one that followed.

Mykola Fomin, the chief engineer, was too careless since the previous failed attempts did not result in any critical consequences. However, he made two significant changes to the experiment this time, about which he did not notify his superiors. This decision became the cause of a disaster.

The engineers did not respond in time to a series of alarms at the beginning of the experiment. According to the team, deputy chief engineer Anatoly Dyatlov insisted on continuing the inspection at all costs. For him, it was a matter of realizing professional ambitions: he wanted to complete the experiment by all means and receive an award from the Soviet authorities. After weak resistance, the team obeyed Dyatlov, hoping he was older and more experienced.

It was already too late when the engineers finally rushed to stop the experiment. It was 01:23:47 on the clock when the first explosion occurred. Three seconds later, the reactor was destroyed. The houses of Prypiat rocked from the blast as locals slept peacefully in their beds. Little did they know that at that moment, their lives changed forever.

The International Nuclear Safety Advisory Group investigated the true causes of the Chornobyl accident.

chapters.title: “The people and the party are united!“
chapters.text:
A dazzling beam of light pierced the night sky of Prypiat. The glow came from a broken nuclear reactor. Its twinkling was strange, frightening, and at the same time, somewhat alluring. It carried an incomparable destructive power.

When the engineers, scalded by steam from the reactor, examined their wounds in shock and found their colleagues covered in blood, the first teams of firefighters arrived. Smoke and dust were everywhere, and a large-scale fire broke out.

Reactor Number Four was gone. In its place was a simmering volcano of uranium fuel and graphite — a radioactive blaze that would prove all but impossible to extinguish. ~ Adam Higginbotham

Adam Higginbotham

Sixty-nine firefighters worked on extinguishing the fire in the first hour after the accident and showed unshakable courage. Despite burns, inability to breathe, and nausea, they made every effort to prevent the fire from spreading to the third power unit. Without them, a much larger nuclear disaster would have shaken the world.

A fire at the third power unit of the Chornobyl nuclear power plant would have left a desert in place of Europe.

First responder firefighters sacrificed their lives to avert disaster. They received a lethal dose of radiation and, sadly, did not manage to attend the ceremony awarding them the title of heroes. The heroes were already dead.

During the first hours after the explosion, the authorities of the nuclear power plant, led by Viktor Bryukhanov, hid in a bunker designed to protect against radiation. They made a terrible mistake that cost all the personnel of the nuclear power plant their health and lives, but they couldn’t accept the horrible reality or take accountability for their actions. Bryukhanov refused to believe in the scale of the catastrophe. He stubbornly insisted that the reactor was intact. None of the team could convince him otherwise. The authorities insisted that the workers supply water to the reactor and reduce its temperature. Because of this senseless act, more than a hundred people spent several hours in the zone of direct radiation. All of them fell ill with radiation sickness, and many died in agony a month later.

chapters.title: “More publicity! More democracy!“
chapters.text:
The day after the accident, the authorities were busy maintaining the prestige of Soviet energy in the international political arena. They did not urgently notify all the inhabitants of Prypiat about the catastrophe and radiation threat. Instead, the authorities were concerned about how to cover up what had happened. The radio broadcasted no news, long-distance communication lines did not work, and the post offices were closed.

The KGB conducted interrogations of personnel at the hospital. They forbade the nuclear power plant workers to report anything, even to their families. The only thing the engineers could convey to their wives was requests to close all the windows at home.

Absurdly, while hospitals continued to receive patients with radiation sickness, life in the city went on as usual. Officials planning measures to keep the incident under wraps peered out of their office windows at mothers walking with babies in strollers. A breath of air saturated with radioactive particles accompanied each of their steps.

The Chornobyl disaster emitted more radiation than the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki combined.

A day and a half passed after the accident at the Chornobyl nuclear power plant before the authorities decided to evacuate the population of Prypiat. It was life-threatening to stay in Prypiat for much longer, so an evacuation had to begin urgently. The charges involved all transport companies in Kyiv and nearby cities. More than 1,200 bus drivers came to Prypiat. Accompanied by police, they were supposed to take 45,000 people out of the danger zone. The authorities also used two motor ships and three trains for the evacuation. More than 4,000 people had to stay in Prypiat because they were the city's critical services employees.

On the radio, a woman announced that all residents must pack their essentials and documents, as everyone must evacuate for three days. Pets were supposed to stay at home. At 2:00 pm on April 27, people locked their apartments and boarded the buses waiting near the entrance. None of them knew they would never see their homes again.

Did you know? As a population center, Prypiat was built shortly before the construction of a nuclear power plant, specifically for the workers of the future Chornobyl station and their families.

chapters.title: “Life has become better; life has become happier“
chapters.text:
The Soviet authorities were still debating whether to reveal the truth about the accident when its consequences had already reached Sweden, Denmark, and Finland. These countries reported that air pollution levels had risen sharply in their territories, probably due to an accident abroad. Soon, panic swept across Europe.

Meanwhile, on the main street of Kyiv, there was an annual solemn procession in honor of May Day. Even though the wind from Chornobyl carried radiation directly to Kyiv and air pollution was already hundreds of times higher than the norm, Moscow authorities still ordered a festive parade on Khreshchatyk. Only when black rain began to pour over Belarus did the authorities evacuate all settlements within 30 kilometers of Chornobyl.

Soviet authorities notified the nation on TV about the radiation danger only after ten days of silence.

As expected, the locals panicked. They urgently began to buy iodine in all its forms. The train stations were overcrowded. Everyone tried to save the children first. Trains, clogged with kids without parents, began to arrive in all remote cities. Relatives looked out for them on the platform.

With the same hope and perseverance, the families of nuclear power plant workers looked for their relatives in hospitals. Those on duty the night of the accident and those who first arrived to put out the fires were secretly taken to Moscow. When the relatives finally managed to find them, they had less than a month to spend together. Many family members donated bone marrow, blood, and skin for transplants to the victims. Alas, nothing helped. Irradiated with a lethal dose of radiation, the workers of Chornobyl died slowly and painfully. Those who lived longer than others watched as their friends and colleagues were buried. They were afraid of the dark and asked to leave the lights on in the wards. Lying awake at night, they wondered when their turn would come.

The accident and the government’s inability to protect the population from its consequences finally shattered the illusion that the USSR was a global superpower. ~ Adam Higginbotham

Adam Higginbotham

chapters.title: “Labor in the USSR is a matter of honor, glory, valor, and heroism“
chapters.text:
Meanwhile, the situation in Chornobyl was getting worse. The temperature in the reactor began to rise sharply. There was a threat of another explosion of even greater power.

It was vital to reduce the release of radiation into the atmosphere. To do this, the pilots flew up in helicopters over the destroyed fourth power unit and dumped tons of lead and boron into the reactor. The mixture of these substances was supposed to cool it down. Crews flew from dawn to dusk for several days, working in shifts. When one pilot's body reached the limit of the allowable radiation dose, another entered the post. Alas, these actions did not have the desired effect.

Another recklessly heroic attempt to save the day was to pump water out of the destroyed reactor — 20 people went to certain death for this. They played the most significant role in eliminating the consequences of the disaster.

However, if you think that Soviet authorities duly rewarded the courage of these people, you are sadly mistaken.

Justice was not in the traditions of the Soviet Union.

Those who were not beneficial to the image of the state found themselves on the margins of society, regardless of their merits. For example, the scientist Valery Legasov, who participated in the aftermath of the accident and received a massive dose of radiation but miraculously survived, was unprofitable to the authorities. He studied the conditions of nuclear safety and spoke at scientific conferences with reports in which he revealed the causes of the accident at the Chornobyl nuclear power plant. His statements undermined the authority of the Soviet nuclear power industry and exposed the fatal mistakes of the leaders, who did everything to make Legasov considered crazy. In the end, they drove him to suicide.

Of course, thousands of people who later became liquidators did not know about this case. For them, the authorities created a completely different narrative. The mass media spread the idea of ​​heroism and unity of a great state through skillful propaganda. Soon everyone began to sincerely believe that they were working for the common good and the prosperity of future generations.

Did you know? Studies estimate that in just a few years, about 600,000 people cleaned up the aftermath of the Chornobyl accident.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The Chornobyl nuclear power plant accident was a solid blow to the environment for decades. Two hundred thousand sq. km were contaminated. Hundreds of small settlements had to be buried, and thousands lost their homes. Many of them also lost their health and lives. Due to the high radiation pollution, cancer incidence has increased significantly in nearby countries.

The Soviet authorities did not inform the population about the incident in time and did not properly take care of their citizens' safety. A different approach and a transparent policy could have significantly minimized damage and casualties. It is noteworthy that twenty years later, no one has claimed responsibility for what happened. Even though the nuclear power plant authorities served time in prison, they continued denying their guilt even on deathbeds.

In 2000, the Ukrainian government completely shut down the Chornobyl nuclear power plant. For safety reasons, in 2016, they erected a new sarcophagus over the destroyed reactor.

Ukraine is currently working on revitalizing the affected territories, supporting environmental protection activities, and creating conditions for safely visiting Prypiat. According to the state plan, by 2065, Chornobyl will become a safe ecological zone.

Try this


• Watch the documentary The Bell of Chernobyl, which contains testimonies of people directly involved in the tragedy.
• Watch the reflection film Chernobyl. Two Colors Of Time “Film 3.”
• Suppose you like computer games, download Crisis in the Kremlin (2017). You can play on behalf of the ruler of the USSR to prevent the accident at the Chornobyl nuclear power plant.

Read it till the end?
